item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is well written, but it doesn't feel at all cohesive and there doesn't seem to be any rhyme or reason to its qualities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am curious to know more about humans that have been affected by this.

Maybe a researcher is accidentally exposed. He would know it would remove his skull and leave him thinking his new form is normal. What would he remember the effect being? If it's changing people it would probably cause some confusion, if it's swapping alternate reality vertebrates then he would have a different story about what it does. Presumably adding a skull.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this SCP for some reason. It's just the right amount of random.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how this object hints at a meaning like others have posted. I also think I can think of a possible origin for it. My guess is that scip is made with the same methods anart is made, but rather then being made with grand artistic intent or social commentary like something from AWCY, it's simply some friend group's in-joke. The in-joke somehow involves skulls and the scip has the characteristics of stereotypical 1960s man because that's the voice that got associated with the skull joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bogleech's opinion: http://www.bogleech.com/scp/6-1754.html

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The paragraphs are oddly place and it detracts from the quality of the scip. Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All i can think of now is skinned furbys, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ten Internets for you. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice. +1.
It doesn't go past my WTF limit( which is admittedly large) because it isn't that stereotypical 1960s guy, but has a poor conversational simulator. It adds to the mystery of where it came from, and for that, I'd like more clues.
Hypothetically, it might have been created in the 1960s by someone or something that, while itself sapient, probably could never have begun to pass a human Turing test. Said creator observed one of those locals with the large bones in their heads, recording its noises, then used it to create this simulacrum. Why? No friggin idea…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Re: green carpet…

What kind of neighbor would he be if he walked on the grass?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Animal subjects affected by SCP-1754-1's smoke are to be contained in standard containment for their species, with appropriate containment modifications to compensate for their physical alterations.

This shouldn't be "animal" but "vertebrate". Animals include everything from sponges to jellies to insects. Unless this thing is also altering things with exoskeletons or no skeletons at all you need to change this for the sake of consistency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea how I misread the first sentence in the description to say "non-sentient" rather than non-sapient as it actually goes. I know the difference between these two, it was just a honest mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is…odd. But it's odd in a good way. I like it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In all fairness, without the smoke effect it'd probably just be an anomalous item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but I kind of tripped on the "genetic sequencing determining ancestor species" thing. I don't know if it quite works like that.

When DNA mutates, it's very unlikely that it will all mutate at once. It's likely that it will be just a small number of affected molecules (just as it's likely that such a mutation will be detrimental, though with enough gazillions of mutations you hit on the occasional one that's beneficial, or at least benign). Anyway, as a result, there will often be plenty of genetic information identical to whatever came before. That's while you'll see random trivia statements such as "pigs and humans share blah-blah% genetic structure". Well, sure, we're closer to each other in our divergence history than we are compared to, say, fish.

The ultimate point being that, whatever happens to the subject affected by the smoke, it involves a distinct change in its DNA that still leaves evidence of its pre-affected state. The significance of this, of course, is beyond me, but there it is. EDIT: One significant conclusion is that the DNA change suggests it isn't merely rearranging physical attributes, like extreme plastic surgery or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's such a grand mixture of "bizarre, but at least it has some connection to reality" and "completely out of left field WTF?!?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Broadly, "sentient" means it can perceive its surroundings and react. "Sapient" means it has a greater awareness, probably including understanding of abstract concepts, complex communication, things like that. This certainly isn't a comprehensive breakdown, of course, but I think it hits the major difference as it pertains to this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In retrospect, yes, "temporal styloid processes" would work even better.

Other than that, though, well done — have my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Speaking not as a biologist, I think it might be a good idea to specify "temporal styloid processes". I of course had to Google the phrase, and it turns out there is more than one styloid process, and they aren't all on the skull. But Photosynthetic should probably have the final say on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As if the effects weren't good enough on their own, the interview really sold it for me. What a hoot! I like to think it's not actually changing organisms with the smoke, but transporting in analogues from whatever whacko 60's-world the voice lives in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The skull stealing aspect is a little over the top for meeee… buuuuut I like the little guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I maintain this thing is hella cute. Would upvote again and light his pipe for him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is neat, and you gave a good explanation of why this skip is not sapient. Really well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aokay, how's that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ I've got a few bones to pick with this article's biology.
- Sorry. The pun was irresistible.

the spurs of the skull's temporal bones

You mean the styloid processes? I know it's a bit sesquipedalian, but I'm willing to bet most people don't know temporal bones have "spurs" at all — or even, possibly, what a temporal bone is — so they're already either skimming it or looking it up. Why not use correct jargon in such a case?

The calcium-rich composition of SCP-1754 accounts for neither the flexibility of the clawed appendages nor the free rotation of the wheels.

This phrasing's kind of awkward: it implies that high calcium content would ordinarily promote flexibility and free rotation. Perhaps "Despite SCP-1754's calcium-rich composition, its clawed appendages are flexible and its wheels rotate freely."

Genetic sequencing of instances of SCP-1754-2 points to divergence not only from their original species' lineage, but from every evolutionary ancestor up to the earliest fishes.

As Kalinin pointed out, this is a little odd. Fortunately, I think it's just a phrasing problem. It is entirely possible to determine the most recent common ancestor of two organisms by comparing DNA sequences; that's how modern taxonomists develop all their cladograms. Perhaps you could rephrase: "Gene sequencing of SCP-1754-2 instances indicates that each instance is no longer a member of its original species, belonging instead to an otherwise-unrecognized group entirely divergent from the vertebrate lineage."

That is what the original sentence implied, for what it's worth: "the earliest fishes" were also the earliest creatures with both skulls and spinal columns (vertebrates). Sister taxa to those creatures (non-vertebrate chordates) include lancelets, which have proto-spinal columns but no heads or skulls, and hagfish, which have skulls but no spinal columns. As conservation of WTF goes, I like the idea of a genetic divergence at that point. :D

Other than that, though, my only complaint is with the conservation-of-WTF issue that Kalinin mentioned: the "howdy-neighbor!" babble feels completely disconnected from the rest of the article. I like the idea generally, and the writing's solid. No vote for now, but if you fix those biological quibbles you'll have my tentative +1, and if you do something about the weirdness-disconnect you'll have a hearty recommendation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was actually with you on the first part. For the health of the mainlist, I don't mind the occasional skip that's merely weird for the sake of weird. Not everything has to be a gigantic epic story. So weird animate skull thing with wheels and pincers that smokes a pipe? Okay, sure. Why not.

The evolution thing…I dunno. Someone with more knowledge of genetics can step in here, but I kind of tripped on the "genetic sequencing determining ancestor species" thing. I don't know if it quite works like that. The thing where it eliminates skulls was at least thematically consistent in its own way, I guess, but at this point it started to feel kind of overly complicated.

And then it talks like a chummy neighbor for no reason. And at that point you blew past my personal threshold for Conservation of WTF. I will occasionally get on board with things what do a random thing, but there's too much lolrandom here. I might get on board with a streamlined version of this, but as it stands I have to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to wonder what the inspiration for this was. And why it won't roll over green carpet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this one, it made me laugh. The effects of SCP-1754-1 are very intriguing; I would kind of like to see a test log with different species of animal introduced to the smoke cloud.

Great article though, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found it funny and creepy enough to upvote, although I think I'd prefer it if a silent skull just rolled itself about the place. Personal preference, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Correct me if I'm wrong. This is a skull with crab pincers and wheels? Someone needs to draw this because it sounds hilarious as all fuck.

All in all, I smiled throughout the article. Take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is sentient, but it's not sapient. It's different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love weird shit like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The article describes this skip as both sentient and non-sentient on two different occassions. So, which one is it?

Nitpick aside, upvoting. It's just so weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very strange and unique

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder if there's anything cohesive behind this, or if it's just different ideas mashed together in hopes of seeing 'omg wtf did i just read'.

I tentatively kind of like it, so I do hope that there's an actual why to this what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's 2am…
Shit
+1
You earned it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it a lot. A nice ratio between strange and creepy.

Some sentences could use a bit if pause in them, like the first sentence in the 2nd paragraph of the description.
